lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Primary versus acquired resistance
We talked about the patients who respond.
Now let's talk about the patients who don't, and the patients who initially do and then stop.
Resistance to checkpoint inhibitors is one of the central problems of the field, and like resistance to any cancer therapy, it comes in two flavors: primary and acquired.
Primary resistance is when a patient is given the drug and never responds.
The tumor grows from the first dose.
About sixty percent of patients across most indications have primary resistance — they get no benefit at all.
We've discussed many of the reasons: low tumor mutational burden, no T-cell infiltrate, loss of MHC class I, an immunosuppressive microenvironment.
Each of these is a way the cancer was never vulnerable to checkpoint blockade in the first place.
Acquired resistance is more poignant.
A patient responds.
The tumor shrinks.
They feel better.
Then, months or years later, the cancer comes back.
Sometimes in the same place; sometimes in a new place.
And often, when you biopsy the recurrent tumor, you find that it has changed.
It is no longer the same tumor that responded.
The known mechanisms of acquired resistance are a rogues' gallery of clever cellular tricks.
Some tumors lose MHC class I expression.
They downregulate the antigen-presentation machinery so that even T cells whose brakes have been released can no longer see the cancer cells.
The white flag is no longer up, but neither is the slideshow.
The T cell walks past a blank wall.
Some tumors mutate beta-2 microglobulin, a key component of MHC class I.
Same effect — no antigen presentation, no T-cell recognition.
Some tumors lose interferon-gamma signaling.
Interferon-gamma is the cytokine that T cells secrete when they're killing, and it has many effects, including upregulating MHC on neighboring cells.
Tumors that mutate the interferon-gamma receptor or its downstream signaling become unable to respond to T-cell signals, and they become invisible.
Some tumors upregulate other checkpoints.
When you block PD-1, the tumor may compensate by displaying ligands for TIM-3, LAG-3, TIGIT, or other inhibitory receptors.
The T cells are released from the PD-1 brake but immediately captured by another brake.
Some tumors recruit more immunosuppressive cells.
Regulatory T cells, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, tumor-associated macrophages — all of these can be upregulated to suppress the responding T cells in new ways.
And some tumors simply lose the neoantigens.

Tumors are heterogeneous; the cells that get killed are the ones that express the immunogenic peptides; the cells that survive and grow are the ones that, by mutation or epigenetic silencing, no longer express them.
It is Darwinian selection inside the tumor, with the immune system as the selector.
The cancer evolves to escape, exactly as it evolved to escape every other treatment we've thrown at it.
The implication is that no single drug, no matter how powerful, is going to be enough.
Even pembrolizumab, with its long, durable responses, eventually loses some patients to acquired resistance.
We have to think in terms of combinations, sequencing, and rational selection.
This is also why understanding resistance mechanisms is one of the hottest research areas in immuno-oncology.
If we can predict which resistance mechanism a given patient is going to develop, we can pre-empt it — combine a second drug from the start that addresses the predicted escape route.
If we can detect acquired resistance early — through circulating tumor DNA, for example, or imaging biomarkers — we can switch therapies before clinical progression.
The field is moving toward a more dynamic, longitudinal view of immune-tumor evolution.
Resistance is, in some ways, the central problem of the next decade.
Checkpoint inhibitors got us into the room.
They have not, by themselves, solved cancer.
Most patients still progress.
The question now is what we do about it.
